author: @Ken00001010
text: As it happens, I just finished reading Robert Sapolsky's book, "Determined" and am spending some time trying to resolve that with Dan Dennett's book, "Freedom Evolves." There are plenty of videos on YouTube by each of them on their ideas. Sapolsky would be more with you because he contends that every thought you take and decision you make is a result of the interaction of your genes and all the events and experiences that have come before. Dennett grants much of that, but holds out for a chaotic process of feedback loops in your brain giving rise to an emergent agent with the power to make decisions. So far, Dennett has not been able to piece together the third person evidence for this first person experience, but very much wants to because he sees Sapolsky's position (which is also held by Sam Harris) as the end of blame and praise. It is a timely question because we will soon have robots that will be having their own thoughts and making their own decisions, and we will have to review our own views about how we justify what we do to them and each other.

author: @selfcorrected-Bobby
text: I've had to try and explain "Had you helped, I would not have had to have  begged" What tense "have had to have begged" and modality it is, is SO confusing and I even made an LLM confused. I've still to resolve this and I'm supposed to be an online English teacher. (unemployed now)

replies.author: @lj8549
replies.text:
The sentence "Had you helped, I would not have had to have begged" is a conditional sentence that expresses a hypothetical situation in the past. The phrase uses the following grammatical elements:

- "Had you helped" is the if-clause (protasis) in the past perfect tense, expressing a condition that was not met.
- "I would not have had to" is the main clause (apodosis) in the conditional perfect tense, indicating the consequence of the unmet condition.
- "have begged" is the perfect infinitive, which indicates an action that was completed in the past.

English modal verbs and their tenses can indeed be tricky, especially when layered with conditionals.
